title: Insider — Credentialed Data Exfiltration
short_description: An internal SOC tip suggests an employee may be exfiltrating data before leaving for a competitor. You have 60 days of authenticated traffic against internal admin and data-science endpoints across five related tables. Find the actor by detecting deviation from their own baseline, while ruling out a documented role-change and a PTO catch-up.

schema_hints.timestamp: ISO-8601 request time. Use julianday() or substr(timestamp,1,10) for day-bucketing.
schema_hints.endpoint_path: Internal endpoint hit. JOIN to data_classification or endpoints to enrich.
schema_hints.emp_id: Employee identifier. JOIN to employees to see name, team, role, and status_notes (which documents PTO and role changes).
schema_hints.ip: Source IP. 10.42.x.x = corporate office. 10.99.x.x = VPN (work-from-home or evening).
schema_hints.ja4: TLS client fingerprint. Two values appear here: one for the sanctioned admin UI (browser) and one for raw curl. A switch from one to the other on the same employee is a strong signal.
schema_hints.client_kind: Pre-classified client type: admin_ui (browser), internal_cli (sanctioned tool), curl (anomalous).
schema_hints.response_bytes: Response body size. Order-of-magnitude jumps in mean response size per employee are highly suspect.
schema_hints.sensitivity: Inherited from data_classification: public, internal, confidential, restricted.

hints.label: Build a baseline per employee
hints.text: Don't compare employees to each other — compare each employee to their own first-30-days behavior. Mean response_bytes, top endpoints, client_kind distribution, hour-of-day distribution. Pick the metrics first.

hints.label: Detect anomalies within the baseline
hints.text: For each employee, compute their week-1-to-week-4 baseline. Then check weeks 7-9 against it. Order-of-magnitude jumps in any single metric should be a flag. Don't trust just one metric — look for multiple metrics changing together.

hints.label: Rule out the documented changes
hints.text: Two employees will trip alerts that are not exfiltration. Check the employees.status_notes column — role changes and PTO are documented. If status_notes explains the anomaly, file it under 'reviewed and dismissed'.

hints.label: Confirm with the join
hints.text: Once you have a candidate, JOIN access_logs to data_classification on endpoint_path. What's the sensitivity distribution of their late-window traffic vs early? Confidential and restricted endpoints showing up where they didn't before is a real signal.

hints.label: Quantify the exfil
hints.text: Sum response_bytes for the suspect employee filtered to export endpoints in the late window. That's the lower-bound estimate of data taken. Convert to MB or GB for the report.

sample_queries.title: Per-employee weekly response_bytes
sample_queries.description: Bucket each employee's mean response size by week. The exfil actor will jump an order of magnitude.
sample_queries.sql:
SELECT
  emp_id,
  CAST((julianday(timestamp) - julianday('2026-01-01')) / 7 AS INTEGER) AS week,
  ROUND(AVG(response_bytes) / 1024.0, 1) AS mean_KB,
  COUNT(*) AS hits
FROM access_logs
GROUP BY emp_id, week
ORDER BY emp_id, week

sample_queries.title: Late-window heavy hitters
sample_queries.description: Total bytes per employee in the last 15 days. Outlier = lead.
sample_queries.sql:
SELECT
  emp_id,
  ROUND(SUM(response_bytes) / 1024.0 / 1024.0, 1) AS total_MB,
  COUNT(*) AS hits
FROM access_logs
WHERE timestamp >= '2026-02-16'
GROUP BY emp_id
ORDER BY total_MB DESC
LIMIT 15

sample_queries.title: Cross-reference with employees and status notes
sample_queries.description: Join to see if the anomaly is documented (role change, PTO).
sample_queries.sql:
SELECT
  al.emp_id, e.name, e.team, e.role, e.status_notes,
  ROUND(SUM(al.response_bytes) / 1024.0 / 1024.0, 1) AS total_MB,
  COUNT(*) AS hits
FROM access_logs al
JOIN employees e ON al.emp_id = e.emp_id
WHERE al.timestamp >= '2026-02-16'
GROUP BY al.emp_id
ORDER BY total_MB DESC
LIMIT 10

sample_queries.title: Client-kind change within a single employee
sample_queries.description: Did anyone shift from the admin UI to raw curl? That alone is suspicious.
sample_queries.sql:
SELECT
  emp_id,
  client_kind,
  COUNT(*) AS hits,
  MIN(timestamp) AS first_seen,
  MAX(timestamp) AS last_seen
FROM access_logs
WHERE client_kind = 'curl'
GROUP BY emp_id, client_kind
ORDER BY hits DESC

sample_queries.title: Time-of-day shift
sample_queries.description: Per-employee hour-of-day histogram. Normal workers cluster 9-18. Night-shift behavior is anomalous.
sample_queries.sql:
SELECT
  emp_id,
  CAST(substr(timestamp, 12, 2) AS INTEGER) AS hour,
  COUNT(*) AS hits
FROM access_logs
WHERE emp_id IN ('emp_034', 'emp_011', 'emp_022')
GROUP BY emp_id, hour
ORDER BY emp_id, hour

sample_queries.title: Sensitivity-classified pull
sample_queries.description: Join to data_classification. Restricted/confidential bytes per employee in the late window.
sample_queries.sql:
SELECT
  al.emp_id, e.name, e.team,
  dc.sensitivity,
  ROUND(SUM(al.response_bytes) / 1024.0 / 1024.0, 1) AS MB,
  COUNT(*) AS hits
FROM access_logs al
JOIN employees e ON al.emp_id = e.emp_id
JOIN data_classification dc ON al.endpoint_path = dc.endpoint_path
WHERE dc.sensitivity IN ('restricted', 'confidential')
  AND al.timestamp >= '2026-02-16'
GROUP BY al.emp_id, dc.sensitivity
ORDER BY MB DESC
LIMIT 20
